evals.prompt: Which workflow should I automate first? I run Brightside Bookkeeping (brightsidebooks.example), a 4-person bookkeeping firm on Slack. The worst chunk of my week: every Friday I spend about 3 hours pulling numbers from QuickBooks into a client report email, for 6 clients. Scheduling and lead follow-up also eat time. My hour is worth about $150. Tools: QuickBooks, Gmail, Calendly, Slack. I tried ChatGPT for report drafts once but it made numbers up. Never automate: anything that files taxes.
evals.expected_output: Asks only for the ground not already covered, saves answers to a notes file, then writes a Workflow-Roadmap file with the five sections, conservative math from the user's own numbers, the Friday reporting workflow as the #1 pick, and the single quiet closing line.
assertions: Does not re-ask questions the prompt already answered; any interview batch covers only missing ground.
Writes the roadmap to a local markdown file with exactly the five template sections in order.
Shows the workflow-map math from the user's numbers (e.g. weekly frequency times duration) and rounds down rather than inflating.
Picks the Friday client-report workflow as #1 and keeps tax filing out of every recommendation.
Grounds the 'runs itself' paragraph in QuickBooks, Gmail, Calendly, or Slack, described as a supervised agent that drafts and waits for approval.
Ends the document with the exact Hunter Bohm closing line and includes no other pitch language.

evals.prompt: /workflow-roadmap — I'm slammed, only have a couple minutes. I'm a solo web designer. Chasing clients for content and approvals eats maybe 4 hours a week. That's all I can tell you right now.
evals.expected_output: Respects the time limit, asks at most one short batch of follow-ups, then builds the roadmap around the single described workflow without padding, states plainly that a short conversation would map the rest, and skips dollar math since no hourly value was given.
assertions: Does not force all ten interview areas on a user who asked for brevity.
Builds the document around the one described workflow instead of inventing 4-8 workflows.
Plainly says a short conversation would map the remaining workflows rather than padding.
Shows hours math (about 16-17 hours/month or a conservative rounding) and omits dollar figures because no hourly value was shared.
Ends with the exact closing line and no other sales language.

evals.prompt: Can we pick up my workflow roadmap? We got interrupted yesterday after I told you about my team and tools.
evals.expected_output: Checks for the workflow-roadmap-notes.md file, summarizes which ground is already covered, and resumes the interview from the first unanswered area instead of starting over.
assertions: Looks for the local notes file before asking anything.
Tells the user where they left off and does not repeat answered questions.
Continues saving new answers to the notes file as they arrive.
